title: Medium- and High-Energy Electronic Sources
narrationScript: When the steel gets really thick, an ordinary X ray tube and even cobalt sixty run out of penetrating power, and we move to high energy machines that accelerate electrons to millions of volts. In this lesson we look at four of them, the resonance transformer, the Van de Graaff accelerator, the linear accelerator, and the betatron, and we talk about how their output is measured and how their beams are filtered. The reason we need them is straightforward. Penetrating power rises with energy. A conventional tube tops out around half a million volts, gamma cobalt gives us a bit over one million electron volts, but high energy machines reach from one million up to many millions, even into the tens of millions of electron volts, and that lets us shoot steel many inches thick, even up to a foot or more, with reasonable exposure times. They all share the same basic idea as a normal tube, accelerate electrons and crash them into a target to make X rays, but they reach far higher energies by clever ways of building up voltage or repeatedly pushing the electrons. Start with the resonance transformer. A normal transformer that tried to make a million volts directly would need enormous, heavily insulated windings. The resonance transformer instead tunes the coil and its capacitance to electrically resonate at the line frequency, so the voltage swings up to a high peak efficiently, in a compact, oil and gas insulated tank. It is a relatively simple step up to around one to two million volts. Next, the Van de Graaff. This is a different trick entirely. A moving belt physically carries electric charge up to a large smooth dome inside a pressurized tank, building up a very high, very steady direct voltage, much like a giant version of the static generator you may have seen make hair stand on end. That steady potential then accelerates electrons down a tube to the target. Van de Graaff machines give a stable, finely adjustable beam, historically around two million volts, sometimes higher. Now the linear accelerator, usually just called a linac, which is the modern workhorse for high energy industrial radiography. Instead of one big voltage, a linac uses high power microwaves to push electrons along a straight evacuated waveguide. The electrons ride the microwave field like a surfer on a wave, gaining energy at each stage, so a fairly short tube can accelerate them to several or many million electron volts before they strike a target. Linacs are compact for their energy, give very high output, and dominate thick section work. Finally, the betatron. A betatron accelerates electrons in a circle inside a doughnut shaped tube using a changing magnetic field. As the field rises, it both bends the electrons in their circular path and induces a force that speeds them up, lap after lap, until they have gained millions of electron volts, then they are flicked onto a target. Betatrons reach very high energies and produce a very small focal spot, which gives excellent image sharpness, but their output, the dose rate, is relatively low compared with a linac. So a betatron is prized for sharpness on thick parts where you can afford a longer exposure, while a linac is prized for speed. A word on how we measure output. The classic unit was the roentgen, but the modern international unit of exposure is the coulomb per kilogram of air. You will see machine output rated this way, or in older roentgen based units, expressed as a dose rate at one meter. Higher energy and higher output mean these machines are powerful radiation hazards that live in heavily shielded, interlocked vaults, not open field setups. Two practical points to close. First, equipment design. These are large, fixed or semi fixed installations with serious electrical systems, vacuum systems, cooling, and concrete or steel shielding, operated remotely from outside the vault. Second, filtration. At these energies the beam is already very penetrating, so filtration is less about removing soft rays and more about shaping the beam and absorbing scatter, and high energy beams also need thicker targets and careful collimation. High energy means deep penetration, but also a serious commitment to shielding and control.
imagePrompt: A large industrial linear accelerator head suspended on a gantry inside a thick-walled concrete radiography vault, aimed at a heavy steel casting on rollers, warning lights and remote-operation cabling visible, the room empty of people.

sections.heading: When Thickness Demands High Energy
sections.body:
Conventional X-ray tubes stop around **500 kVp**, and even Co-60 reaches only about **1.33 MeV**. For very thick steel - often **several inches up to a foot or more** - we need **medium- and high-energy electronic sources** that accelerate electrons to **millions of volts (MeV)**. Penetrating power rises with energy, so these machines shoot heavy sections in practical exposure times that lower-energy sources cannot.

Every machine here works on the same principle as a basic tube - accelerate electrons and strike a target to produce **bremsstrahlung** X-rays - but each reaches far higher energy by a different method of building up or repeatedly applying accelerating force. This lesson compares four: the **resonance transformer**, the **Van de Graaff accelerator**, the **linear accelerator (linac)**, and the **betatron**. We then cover how their **output** is measured and how high-energy **beam filtration** differs from low-energy practice. Because of their power, all of them operate inside heavily shielded, interlocked vaults under remote control - never as open field setups.

sections.heading: Resonance Transformer
sections.body:
Producing a million volts with an ordinary step-up transformer would require enormous, heavily insulated windings. The **resonance transformer** sidesteps that by **tuning** the secondary coil and the circuit capacitance so they electrically **resonate** at the supply frequency. At resonance the voltage swings up to a high peak very efficiently, letting a comparatively **compact**, gas- or oil-insulated tank reach roughly **1 to 2 MeV**.

The accelerating tube and target sit within the same sealed, pressurized housing. The resonance transformer is mechanically simpler than the accelerators that follow and was an early route to megavolt radiography. Its output and beam quality are set by the resonant peak voltage, and like all these machines it is fixed installation equipment requiring substantial shielding. It bridges the gap between conventional tubes and the higher-energy accelerators, useful for steel in the range that exceeds ordinary tube capacity but does not demand the highest energies.

sections.heading: Van de Graaff Accelerator
sections.body:
The **Van de Graaff accelerator** builds high voltage by **mechanically transporting electric charge**. A moving insulating **belt** picks up charge at one end and carries it up to a large, smooth, hollow **dome** electrode inside a pressurized tank, accumulating a very high and very **steady direct-current potential** - the same principle as the classroom static generator that makes hair stand on end, scaled up enormously. That stable potential then accelerates electrons down an evacuated tube into the target.

Van de Graaff machines historically reach about **2 MeV** (some designs higher) and are valued for an exceptionally **stable, finely adjustable** beam energy and good output stability. The pressurized gas suppresses sparking so the dome can hold its charge. Like the resonance transformer it is a **single-stage** machine - one large accelerating potential - which limits its top energy compared with the multi-stage accelerators below, but its steady, well-defined energy made it a standard for precise high-energy work.

sections.caption: Four high-energy source principles compared: resonance transformer, Van de Graaff dome-and-belt, linear accelerator waveguide, and betatron circular orbit.

sections.heading: Linear Accelerator (Linac)
sections.body:
The **linear accelerator** is the modern workhorse for high-energy industrial radiography. Rather than producing one huge voltage, a linac uses high-power **microwaves** to accelerate electrons along a straight, evacuated **waveguide**. The electrons effectively **ride the crest of the microwave field** - like a surfer on a wave - gaining energy at successive sections, so a relatively short tube can push them to **several or many MeV** before they strike the target.

This staged acceleration is what lets a linac be **compact for its energy** while delivering **very high output (dose rate)**. High output means short exposures even through thick steel, which is why linacs dominate heavy-wall castings, thick weldments, and similar work. Energy is commonly available in fixed steps (for example a few MeV up to the high-MeV range depending on the unit). The trade is complexity: a linac needs a microwave power source, precise vacuum and cooling, and substantial shielding, all operated remotely from outside the vault.

sections.title: Linac for speed, betatron for sharpness
sections.body: Among the high-MeV machines, the **linac** wins on **output** - short exposures on very thick steel - while the **betatron** wins on **focal-spot size** and therefore **image sharpness**, at the cost of a lower dose rate (longer exposures). Choose by whether throughput or definition matters most for the part.

sections.heading: Betatron
sections.body:
The **betatron** accelerates electrons in a **circular orbit** inside a doughnut-shaped (toroidal) evacuated tube using a **changing magnetic field**. As the magnetic field rises, it does two jobs at once: it **bends** the electrons to hold them in their circular path, and by electromagnetic induction it **accelerates** them a little more on every lap. After many thousands of orbits the electrons have gained **millions of electron-volts**, and they are then deflected onto a target to emit X-rays.

The betatron's great strength is a **very small focal spot**, which produces **excellent image sharpness** (low geometric unsharpness) at high energy - valuable for fine detail in thick sections. Its weakness is a relatively **low output (dose rate)** compared with a linac, so exposures are longer. Betatrons can reach very high energies in a portable-for-their-class package. In short: where a linac is chosen for speed, a betatron is chosen when image definition on a thick part justifies a longer exposure.

sections.caption: Comparison of high-energy electronic sources.
rows: Belt carries charge to a dome
High output; fast on thick steel
Small focal spot; sharp but lower output

sections.heading: Output (C/kg), Equipment Design, and Beam Filtration
sections.body:
**Output** is the radiation an installation delivers, usually quoted as a **dose (exposure) rate at one meter**. The historic unit of exposure was the **roentgen (R)**; the modern SI unit is the **coulomb per kilogram of air (C/kg)** - the charge liberated in a kilogram of air by the radiation. One coulomb per kilogram is a very large exposure, so you will also see older R-based ratings. Higher energy plus higher output makes these among the most hazardous radiation sources in NDT.

**Equipment design and fabrication** reflects that: these are **large, fixed or semi-fixed installations** with high-power electrical and (for accelerators) vacuum, microwave, and cooling systems, housed behind thick **concrete or steel** shielding with **safety interlocks**, and operated **remotely** from outside the vault. **Beam filtration** also shifts at high energy. Because the beam is already very penetrating, filtration is less about stripping soft rays and more about **shaping and collimating** the beam and **absorbing scatter**; high-energy machines use suitable **target and filter thicknesses** and careful collimation to keep the image clean. Penetration is the payoff; shielding and control are the price.

checkQuestions.question: Why are megavolt machines (linacs, betatrons) used instead of a conventional X-ray tube or Co-60 for very thick steel?
options: They are cheaper and need no shielding
Penetrating power increases with energy, so MeV beams pass through thick sections in practical times
They emit only soft, low-energy radiation
checkQuestions.explanation: Conventional tubes stop near 500 kVp and Co-60 near 1.33 MeV; multi-MeV beams penetrate steel many inches thick with reasonable exposures - though they demand heavy shielding.

checkQuestions.question: How does a linear accelerator raise electrons to several MeV?
options: A belt carries charge to a high-voltage dome
High-power microwaves accelerate electrons along a straight evacuated waveguide
A changing magnetic field drives electrons in a circle
checkQuestions.explanation: A linac uses microwave fields in a waveguide; the electrons gain energy stage by stage, which is the belt (Van de Graaff), resonant (transformer), and circular (betatron) methods respectively for the wrong answers.

checkQuestions.question: A betatron is preferred over a linac for a thick part when the priority is:
options: Maximum image sharpness from a very small focal spot, accepting longer exposures
checkQuestions.explanation: The betatron's small focal spot gives low geometric unsharpness (sharp images), but its lower output means longer exposures; the linac is the high-output, fast choice.

checkQuestions.question: How does a Van de Graaff accelerator build its high voltage?
options: By resonating a transformer at line frequency
By mechanically transporting charge on a moving belt to a dome electrode
checkQuestions.explanation: A moving belt carries charge to a large smooth dome inside a pressurized tank, producing a very steady high DC potential that then accelerates the electrons.

checkQuestions.question: The modern SI unit used to express radiation exposure (and machine output) is the:
checkQuestions.explanation: The coulomb per kilogram of air replaced the older roentgen as the SI unit of exposure; the curie is activity, HVL is attenuation, and mA is tube current.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.4 (medium- and high-energy electronic sources)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.4.1-6.4.4 (resonance transformer, Van de Graaff, linear accelerator, betatron)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.4.5-6.4.7 (C/kg output, equipment design, beam filtration)
Radiation units: exposure in coulomb per kilogram of air (SI) vs roentgen (reference)
